items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.q: Why does the cognitive approach use INFERENCE?
items.correct: Mental processes cannot be directly observed, so they are inferred from behaviour.
items.misconception: Because cognitive psychologists guess instead of measuring.
items.why: Inference is a methodological necessity, not a weakness of rigour. Students treat it as vague guessing. It means drawing conclusions about unobservable processes from observable behaviour.

items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.q: Bartlett's 'War of the Ghosts' study supports which cognitive concept?
items.why: Correct answer: Schema. “Capacity” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which scanning method is most associated with cognitive neuroscience?
items.why: It's fMRI / PET. “EEG only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Bartlett publish 'War of the Ghosts'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1932. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
